nativeRange.habitat: Large river channels, flooded forests, and tributaries of the Congo Basin; prefers areas with submerged vegetation, woody debris, and moderate to strong current

decorPreferences: no live plants (will consume and uproot)
robust external canister filtration for heavy bioload

diet.notes: Obligate plant-eater; will consume and destroy virtually all soft-leaved aquatic vegetation. Diet should be based on plant matter: blanched vegetables (cucumber, zucchini, spinach, lettuce, kale), spirulina wafers, algae-based pellets, and fresh vegetable matter. Romaine lettuce and blanched courgette attached to clips are widely accepted. Some animal protein (earthworms, sinking pellets) accepted opportunistically but plant matter must dominate. Wild fish feed heavily on aquatic macrophytes and terrestrial leaf litter entering floodplain habitats.

goodWith: large robust Congo Basin cichlids (Boulengerochromis, large Cyphotilapia)
large plecos and armored catfish (Synodontis species)
large barbs and danios of similar size
large robust tetras such as Congo Tetra in juvenile stage only
other large non-aggressive fish too big to be harassed
avoidWith: any live aquatic plants (will be consumed)
small fish (will be eaten or badly harassed at adult size)
dwarf cichlids and peaceful small species
conspecifics unless in very large systems (territorial aggression at maturity)
slow-moving or timid species unable to compete for food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image showing juvenile banding pattern; check current availability on FishBase species page.
Aquarium specimen showing vivid juvenile orange-red body with six dark vertical bars.

summary: A large, striking characiform from the Congo Basin reaching 75 cm. Juveniles display vivid orange-red coloration with six bold black vertical bars that fade with age. A dedicated plant-destroyer suited only to specialist large-fish setups of 1000 L or more.
careNotes: The Six-Banded Distichodus is one of the largest members of the family Distichodontidae, routinely reaching 55–75 cm in aquarium conditions and demanding a tank of at least 1000 L with a footprint of 300 cm or more. Despite being sold at 8–12 cm as an attractive juvenile \"community fish,\" it rapidly outgrows standard aquaria and becomes increasingly aggressive toward tankmates as it matures. Juveniles display spectacular orange-red to red-orange body coloration marked with six crisp black vertical bars; this pattern fades progressively with age, with adults often appearing drab olive-grey with reduced or entirely absent barring — a classic juvenileColorPhase species where young fish bear little resemblance to their adult form. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–15, kH 1–8) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), replicating the blackwater to clearwater Congo tributaries. Temperature 23–28°C. The species is an obligate herbivore and will systematically consume and uproot every live plant in the aquarium; hardscape-only setups with large smooth boulders and heavy driftwood are strongly recommended. Excellent filtration is essential due to the heavy bioload produced by large specimens. This species should not be purchased by hobbyists without the infrastructure to house a specimen approaching 75 cm — it is irresponsible to acquire this fish for a standard community setup.
breedingNotes: Not recorded in the aquarium hobby. Breeding in captivity is considered extremely unlikely given the space requirements for adult-sized specimens. Wild spawning behavior is not well documented but the species is presumed to be an egg-scatterer following the general characiform pattern. No captive breeding protocols exist.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Distichodus sexfasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 75 cm TL), temperature range (23–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (2–15 dGH), native range (Congo Basin, DRC, Angola, Zambia), herbivore diet requirements, minimum tank size (1000 L recommended for adults), juvenile color phase description (orange-red with six black bars fading with age), aggression at maturity, and notes on unsuitability for community tanks.

additional.name: FishBase — Distichodus sexfasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (75 cm TL confirmed), taxonomy (Distichodontidae, Characiformes), native range (Congo and Kasai river systems), diet classification (herbivore, feeds on aquatic plants), and temperature tolerance. Confirmed Congo Basin distribution across DRC, Republic of Congo, Angola, and Zambia.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Distichodus sexfasciatus species profile
additional.notes: Provided hobbyist-oriented husbandry guidance including notes on juvenile color phase (vivid banding fades to dull in adults), unsuitability for planted tanks, recommended tank companions (large robust African species), and specialist-only advisory context. Noted species is frequently sold tiny in trade and quickly outgrows community setups.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Large African Fish Species Guide
additional.notes: Referenced for general guidance on large African characiforms in the hobby trade, food acceptance (algae wafers, spirulina, blanched vegetables), and context on specialist large-fish keeping requirements. No dedicated D. sexfasciatus page available; general African species guide supplemented species-specific data.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Congo Basin Giants: Keeping Large Characiformes
additional.notes: Supplementary reference for Congo Basin biotope context, aggression at maturity, companion species selection, and notes on the trade practice of selling large-growing African species as juveniles without adequate disclosure of adult size requirements.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Adult size requirements (75 cm specimens requiring 1000 L+) make captive reproduction effectively impossible in hobby settings. Egg-scattering spawning behavior inferred from family traits but not observed in aquaria.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 75cm; plant-destroying African herbivore characiform. Needs 1000L+ tank. Specialist large-fish setup.
